So the point that I was making is that essentially your pain threshold and your action threshold very inversely. In other words, as pain goes up, action threshold drops. By raising someone's pain, by having someone understand what their real pain is, waking them up out of denial, the impact it has is it lowers their action threshold. Because when someone is really in pain, they act. Even if they're really not the sort of person that normally takes action very quickly, if they really are feeling pain, they're really discomfort and discomfort and they project it on, don't see getting any better. And that's the key. They project it out and don't see themselves getting out of pain anytime soon. They will take action. I told you a story about my dad in this train. Let me tell you a different story. It's a story about a dentist visit I once had. I had this whole issue with like my teeth. It's one tooth that was just thrown bad for years and years and it was a root canal that turned into a failed root canal that turned into a cap and then that didn't work. Anyway, I found myself in a situation where I had to put this like sort of industrial strength temporary crown on while I was preparing to do an implant. So they had done the implant, they put the screw in a dental implant, right? They had a temporary crown over and that crown had kept cracking and re-cracking and it was driving me crazy. So I said, go out and get me the best crown ever, the industrial strength. And the guy did that. He went out and got it made in the lab. It was beautiful, it was real porcelain, but made of some titanium underneath. It built to last, fit like a glove, the whole thing, right? Anyway, it was so great this temporary and six months later, I had to go back and get the actual permanent screwed in. This was just sitting over my screw. I get it to permanent. Let me ask you a question. Do you think I ever went back and got the permanent? No. For years and years, I said, why should I go back and get a permanent tooth put in over the screw? This tooth feels fine. I'm not in pain. What's the point of taking action? And when I had gone to the dentist, finally, my wife, baby, you know, eventually, as many years ago, right? She, my new wife, she forced me to get my, all my, you know, health issues, I'll go to the dentist. When I went to the dentist, the guy says, oh, I see, I see this tooth. Wow. He says, how long has it been? I tell the guy this story. It's been enough for six years. He starts laughing. He goes, well, you're really going to get this thing out. And he goes, you know, you'll cost you $4,000. I got to hack that thing. And he makes it like, like, what the f***, my God, I'll think about it. Now, let me just tell you this. You think I ever would actually take it out because a dentist said, yeah, you know, you really got to get this out. It's going to cost you $4,000. No way. No way. I'm not doing that. But here's what the dentist could have said to me. He goes, wow, tell me about this tooth. How long has this been sitting there? Huh? And he, you know, he, I say, well, it feels great to have no pain. He goes, Jordan, I understand that the tooth is not hurting me right now. But let me say this, I've been doing this for a long time now, and I've seen the situation before. You have a great temporary in there, but one day, you know, boom, it just suddenly cracks that you travel around the world during all these countries, just to match yourself in Africa, you know, and it cracks your immortal pain right now. You can't eat, you can't function. What do you do then? Have some witch doctor fix your tooth? You know, so you're going to future paste my pains, Jordan, believe me, I understand that you're not feeling it right now. But this is a disaster in the making. Let's get this done. Believe me, you'll be glad you did. Only a couple thousand dollars, and you'll be set for life in this thing. Does that sound fair enough? Now, I would say, yeah, why not? Because in other words, he wakes up, my pain, I was in denial. See, the fact that wasn't, I had come to accept the fact that I didn't have a perfect situation, dentively, in my mouth, right? That I'm having a temporary, it's not really meant to be there for everybody. It's a very good one. So I wasn't hurting. So because it wasn't hurting me, I went into denial about it. I'm like, oh, it's not so bad. It's fine. It's good. It looks good, feels good. But meanwhile, in it, there was actually a little infection growing there. Sometimes I have a bad taste of it. It wasn't perfect. But I come to accept the situation. All a dentist had to do was, by the way, wake that up in me that it was really a problem in health. Here's how it happened. When I went for some other issue in my mouth, another dentist said to me, what are you? Are you crazy? Do you understand? Do you know that there's got to be, when you have a temporary in there, there's got to be a low level infection underneath. I understand it's probably makes you tired right now. Although as soon as it's probably making you tired, he didn't say it's made of, you know, that it could do this is probably making you tired. You probably don't realize it. You become accustomed to it. You really got to get that done. I was getting it done the next day. Pain is the motivator that makes us take action. Don't ever forget that. People walk around all day long in denial because it's not because we're weak or stupid. It's just how human beings are. After a while, we just, in order to move through life feeling good, we're forced to just say, you know what? It's okay. We're okay as we are, even when we're not. And because of that, we push down any need we have to really take action. I needed to take action, but I pushed it down and was standing pat. But as soon as someone understood the least bit about women's case, I think they were really a great salesperson. As much as they really understood, like they were able just to communicate naturally. She didn't communicate. She's this woman. She was able to explain to me that, let me take me to real ramifications on here. This is a big deal. You're in denial right now, pal, but you need to get this done. Here are the problems. Here are the cons. You get it? And once she did that, the decision became a no brainer. I was there the next day and another dentist, boom, getting a big hack down and having a permanent date. That's it. That's how sales really works. When people need something, but they're not feeling any short term in the moment discomfort, they are buyers in power. They feel no need to take action to change. When people buy things, it's usually to fill, you know, most cases, 90% of the cases, they're filling some sort of need that's causing them to feel out of control or not good about something, or imperfect or just, you know, in some way, in, or just really in pain if it's actually a real pain in the face. But the point is people do spy to get back in control, to feel better about their life, to feel like they have a clear path forward. When you wake that up and make that clear to someone who's in denial, it's miraculously effective at getting them off the mark to say yes.
